# Rivian Kanban

Rivian Kanban is a facilities-management Kanban board for tracking work orders: a
drag-and-drop web UI for humans, an MCP server for AI agents, and Slack ticket intake, all
over one audited service layer.

This app exposes an MCP server at the `/mcp` path (Streamable HTTP, stateless mode),
authenticated with a per-user bearer token shaped `rkb_...`. Connect an AI agent to it and it
can read the board and (with a write-scoped token) drive card lifecycle through task-shaped
tools.

## Use this app's own origin -- do not assume localhost

Use the SAME origin you fetched this file from as the base URL for everything below. If you
loaded `https://kanban.example.com/llms.txt`, then `<origin>` is `https://kanban.example.com`
and the MCP endpoint is `https://kanban.example.com/mcp`. Substitute your real `<origin>`
everywhere it appears; never hard-code `localhost`. (Local-from-source dev is the one
exception -- see "Local development" at the end.)

## Setup -- guide the human through these steps

You (the agent) orchestrate; the human holds the secret. Do NOT ask the human to paste the
token into this chat. Walk them through:

1. Mint a token. Open `<origin>/settings` -> Service tokens -> New token. Pick scope `read`
   for a summarizer/reporter, `read_write` for an agent that acts. The raw `rkb_...` value is
   shown exactly once -- the human copies it there.
2. Write the MCP config for them, with a placeholder. Add this block to the agent's MCP config
   (e.g. `.mcp.json` for Claude, the equivalent for your client). Replace `<origin>` with the
   app's origin, and tell the human to replace `<PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE>` with the token they
   copied:

   {
     "mcpServers": {
       "rivian-kanban": {
         "url": "<origin>/mcp",
         "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer <PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE>" }
       }
     }
   }

3. The human fills in the token themselves and reloads the client. Smoke-test by listing tools
   (e.g. `whoami`).

## SECURITY -- read this

- The bearer token is a secret. The human MUST paste it into their own config file themselves.
  It must NEVER be shared with, pasted into, or shown to the assistant/chat.
- Your job as the agent is to write the config with the `<PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE>` placeholder
  and tell the human to fill it in. Do not offer to hold, echo, or store the token.
- If a human pastes a token to you anyway, tell them to revoke it (`<origin>/settings` ->
  Service tokens) and mint a new one -- a leaked token is game over until revoked.

## Local development

When running the app locally from source, the web UI is served by Vite (default port 5173) and
the API + MCP by the server (default port 3000), and the Vite dev proxy forwards `/api` but NOT
`/mcp`. So in local dev only, point the MCP client at `http://localhost:3000/mcp` (the server
port), not the Vite port. In every real deployment the server serves both the UI and `/mcp` on
one origin, so `<origin>/mcp` is correct.

Future: an OAuth 2.0 device-code flow could replace the manual paste so the token never leaves
the token endpoint; today the human-in-the-loop paste is the safe, simple flow.
